sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
असंशयं महाबाहो मनो दुर्निग्रहं चलम् ।
अभ्यासेन तु कौन्तेय वैराग्येण च गृह्यते ॥ ३५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
asaṁśayaṁ mahā-bāho mano durnigrahaṁ calam |
abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇa ca gṛhyate || 35 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Lord Śrī Kṛṣṇa said: O mighty-armed son of Kuntī, it is undoubtedly very difficult to curb the restless mind, but it is possible by suitable practice and by detachment.
Lord Krishna said: O mighty-armed son of Kunti, what you say is correct; the mind is indeed very difficult to restrain. But by practice and detachment, it can be controlled.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1936 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The difficulty of controlling the obstinate mind, as expressed by Arjuna, is accepted by the Personality of Godhead. But at the same time He suggests that by practice and detachment it is possible.

positions.rendering: by suitable practice and by detachment
abhyāsa = repetition of one cognition on a single object of concentration; vairāgya = absence of attachment to objects of sense, seen and unseen
abhyāsa = concentration on the self again and again; vairāgya = dispassion towards objects other than the self

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Sankara and Ramanuja both define the dyad sharply. Sankara's reading is more general (any single object); Ramanuja's reading is more devotional (the self / the divine). Patañjali Yoga Sūtra 1.12 codifies the same dyad — abhyāsa-vairāgyābhyāṁ tan-nirodhaḥ. The operational claim — that the answer requires BOTH terms, not either alone — is preserved across all readings.

literal_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: without doubt, O mighty-armed, the mind is hard to restrain and restless. But it is grasped, O son of Kuntī, by practice and by dispassion.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.35 is the two-handed answer. Asaṁśayam — without doubt — concedes that the mind is durnigraham (hard to restrain) and calam (restless). The 'tu' (but) introduces the operational answer: the mind is gṛhyate (grasped, restrained) by abhyāsa (practice — repeated, sustained, returning) AND vairāgya (dispassion — non-attachment to objects of sense, non-clinging to the fruits of the mind's wandering). Both terms are technical and both are required. Patañjali's Yoga Sūtra 1.12 (composed centuries after the Gita) gives the same dyad — abhyāsa-vairāgyābhyāṁ tan-nirodhaḥ. Sankara's reading: by abhyāsa (the repetition of one cognition on a particular object of mental concentration) and by vairāgya (absence of attachment to sense objects, seen and unseen) the mind is restrained. Ramanuja's reading: by abhyāsa (concentration on the self again and again) and vairāgya (dispassion towards objects other than the self) the mind is gripped firmly. Both traditions preserve the BOTH of the answer: not practice alone, not dispassion alone, but both. The verse is pivotal in chapter 6.
